title: Declare your first variable
instruction:
Declare a variable: `type name = value;`

```cpp
int score = 95;       // integer
double pi = 3.14;    // decimal
bool active = true;  // true/false
char grade = 'A';    // single character (single quotes!)
```

Run this — four types, four variables.
successMessage: true prints as 1 and false as 0 — that's how C++ booleans work. Use boolalpha to print 'true'/'false': cout << boolalpha << active. One of C++'s quirks.

title: Create your own variables
instruction:
Declare four variables:
- `int age` — your age
- `double height` — in metres (e.g. 1.75)
- `char initial` — first letter of your name
- `bool isLearning` — set to `true`

Then print each one.
hint: int age = 25; double height = 1.75; char initial = 'A'; bool isLearning = true;

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO with your declarations.

successMessage: Four typed variables — each holds exactly its type. Try assigning a string to an int and watch the compiler refuse. That protection is C++'s greatest strength.

title: Reassigning variables
instruction:
Declare once with the type, then reassign without the type keyword:

```cpp
int count = 0;
count = 10;     // reassign — no 'int' needed
count += 5;     // shorthand: count = count + 5
count++;        // increment by 1
```
successMessage: count-- decrements by 1. +=, -=, *=, /= are compound operators. count++ is post-increment — used so often it gave its name to the language: 'C with one increment'.

title: const — values that don't change
successMessage: const = read-only forever after initialization. Game engines store constants for gravity, speed of light, max players. The compiler prevents accidental modification.

title: auto — type inference
instruction:
`auto` lets the compiler infer the type from the initialiser:

```cpp
auto x = 42;      // int
auto y = 3.14;    // double
auto s = "hello"; // const char*
```

The type is still fixed — `auto` just saves typing it. Prefer explicit types in learning; use `auto` for complex iterator types in real code.
successMessage: auto infers the type at compile time — the type is still fixed, just written for you. Use it for iterators (auto it = vec.begin()), lambdas, and complex template types in real C++ code.

title: Variable scope
instruction:
Variables exist only within their `{...}` block — this is **scope**.

A variable in an inner block shadows an outer one with the same name (usually a bad idea).
successMessage: Outer x=10, inner x=99 — the inner one shadowed the outer. After the block closes, the outer x=10 is visible again. Shadowing is valid but confusing — avoid in real code.

title: Build a profile
instruction:
No starter code. Write everything from scratch.

Declare:
- `const string LANGUAGE = "C++"` (you'll need `#include <string>`)
- `string name` — your name
- `int year` — current year
- `auto chaptersCompleted = 1`

Print: `"Alex is learning C++ in 2024. Chapter 1 done!"`
successMessage: const for constants, auto for inference, explicit types for clarity. That mix of tools is real C++ code. Chapter 2 done!
